title: Pear tarte tatin
subtitle: Caramelised pears in a deep amber butter caramel, topped with puff pastry and inverted
excerpt: Pears cooked in a dark butter caramel, topped with a puff pastry lid and baked, then inverted so the caramel-coated fruit sits on top. The caramel is made directly in the ovenproof pan; the pastry lid is the base once it is flipped. Conference or Comice pears hold their shape through the bake better than soft varieties. The total process takes about 1 hour.
sourceNotes: Tarte Tatin was supposedly invented accidentally at the Hotel Tatin in Lamotte-Beuvron in the 1880s when a tarte was placed in the oven upside-down. The dish is documented in Escoffier and became a staple of French bistro cooking. Delia Smith popularised the pear version in Britain through 'Delia's Complete Cookery Course' (1982). The technique of building the caramel directly in the ovenproof pan is consistent across all serious French sources.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The caramelised pears in the pan can be cooked up to 2 hours ahead and left in the pan. Lay the pastry over them just before baking.

glossaryTerms.definition: The caramel for a tarte tatin is built directly in the ovenproof pan rather than separately. Butter and sugar are cooked together over direct heat until the sugar melts and turns a deep amber colour (around 160 to 170 degrees C). The fruit is added to this caramel in the same pan, which coats and begins to soften it, before the pastry lid goes on top. After baking, inverting the tart transfers the caramel from the base of the pan to the top surface of the fruit. If the caramel is too light it will be sweet without depth; if too dark it will be bitter.

content.text: needs to reach a deep amber before the pears go in: pale golden caramel tastes flat and sweet. Use a sugar thermometer if you have one; if not, the colour is the guide. The moment the caramel turns the colour of dark honey, add the pears.

Use a heavy ovenproof frying pan or a dedicated tarte tatin dish with high sides. The pan must go from the stovetop directly into the oven, so check that the handle is oven-safe. When inverting, use a plate larger than the pan and move quickly and confidently.

items.prepNote: medium-sized Conference or Comice, peeled, halved and cored

content.text: Make the caramel and cook the pears

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Put the 150 g caster sugar in a heavy, 26 cm ovenproof frying pan over medium heat. Cook without stirring until the sugar melts and turns a deep amber colour, about 8 to 10 minutes. Add the 60 g butter and swirl to combine. Add 1 tsp vanilla extract. Arrange the pear halves cut-side up in the caramel, packing them tightly as they will shrink during cooking. Cook on the stovetop for 8 to 10 minutes until the pears are just starting to soften and the caramel is bubbling up around them.

Cut the puff pastry sheet into a circle slightly larger than the pan. Lay it over the pears and tuck the edges down inside the pan around the fruit. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the pastry is puffed and deep golden brown, and feels firm and hollow when tapped in the centre. Remove from the oven and leave to rest for 10 minutes.

Place a large, lipped plate or board over the pan. Holding both firmly together, invert in one confident movement. Lift the pan away; if any pears have stuck, reposition them with a spoon. Serve warm, with creme fraiche or vanilla ice cream.

items.problem: Caramel crystallised and turned grainy before it coloured
items.solution: The sugar was stirred or disturbed before it fully melted. Cook without stirring; swirl the pan if needed rather than using a spoon. If crystallisation starts, add 1 tbsp water and continue heating gently.

items.problem: Pears released so much juice the caramel became watery
items.solution: The pears were overripe. Use firm Conference or Comice pears. If juice does accumulate, tip the pan carefully to one side to spoon off most of the liquid before adding the pastry.

items.problem: Caramel stuck to the pan and pears tore on inversion
items.solution: The tart was inverted too quickly. A 10-minute rest after baking allows the caramel to cool slightly and contract, making release easier. If it still sticks, return the pan to low heat for 30 seconds to loosen.
